value.Title: Meta Signs 850 MW of New Clean Energy Purchase Deals Across U.S.
value.Overview: ESG Today, RENEWABLE ENERGY NEWS & US NEWS, 261words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK3-MDS3-RS4X-44N0-00000-00Meta Signs 850 MW of New Clean Energy Purchase Deals Across U.S.2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:07ZLexisNexisRENEWABLE ENERGY NEWS & US NEWSMeta Signs 850 MW of New Clean Energy Purchase Deals Across U.S.Kenny Fisher

Link to Image

Renewable energy producer DESRI and Meta announced the signing of new power purchase agreements (PPAs),providing the tech giant with 850 MW of clean energy capacity from solar and battery storage projects across the U.S.

Meta, the owner of Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp, ranked as the largest corporate clean energy offtaker globally in 2025, according to a recent report by BloombergNEF, contracting 10.24 GW in the year. The company has set targets to reach net-zero emissions across its value chain by 2030, and to continue to match 100% of the electricity used in its data centers and offices with renewable energy.

Hy Martin, Chief Development Officer of DESRI said:

"These projects support Meta's energy commitments and they will generate significant economic development for local rural economies across the country. Our relationship with large companies like Meta is a cornerstone of DESRI's long-term strategy of delivering cost-effective, reliable energy projects to power the U.S. economy's growth."

The new agreements include 500 MW in Oklahoma, 200 MW in Texas and 150 MW in Mississippi. The new agreements expand Meta's contracted capacity with DESRI to approximately 2,575 MW of solar and battery storage projects across nine states, the companies said.

Amanda Yang, Head of Clean and Renewable Energy at Meta said:

"DESRI has been a valued partner as we work to bring new energy to grids across the United States. This expanded partnership - now spanning nine states - reflects our commitment to supporting the communities where we operate."

value.Title: Nissan Shelves $500 Mn US EV Plan as Demand Slows Post Subsidy Cuts

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JG9-YPV3-SBPS-G0V1-00000-00Nissan Shelves $500 Mn US EV Plan as Demand Slows Post Subsidy Cuts2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:07ZLexisNexisNissan Shelves $500 Mn US EV Plan as Demand Slows Post Subsidy CutsNew Delhi


May 01 --

Japanese automaker Nissan Motor Co. will scrap its plans to produce electric vehicles at its vehicle assembly plant in Canton, Mississippi, company officials said. The decision was associated with sluggish electric vehicle (EV) sales following the end of United States (US) government subsidies for EV purchases.



Nissan made the latest decision after initially announcing in 2022 that it would invest 500 million dollars to manufacture electric vehicles (EVs) at the Canton plant. At the time, the automaker aimed to increase the proportion of EVs to more than 40 pct of its vehicle sales in the United States by fiscal 2030. The company now plans to expand production of other vehicle models at the plant.

An official at Nissan North America Inc. said that the decision was made in light of market conditions, demand, and a review of Nissan's strategy. The official added that the United States is a key market and that the company will continue to focus on it as a foundation for revenue and growth.
Policy Shifts, Rising Costs Threaten US EV Growth Trajectory
Earlier research suggests that the US EV market could face a slowdown in adoption due to recent executive orders. According to a report by Wood Mackenzie, these policy shifts are expected to weigh on future growth. Senior leadership at Wood Mackenzie indicated that plug-in EV adoption could decline significantly, with projections falling from 32% to 23% by 2030.

The report further highlights that tightening of the 30D tax credit could dampen EV battery demand, slow renewable energy installations, and weaken demand for energy storage systems. While the base-case forecast still projects annual U.S. EV and energy storage system (ESS) battery demand to exceed 500 GWh by 2030, a more pessimistic scenario places this figure closer to 400 GWh.

In addition, changes in the sourcing of imported raw materials—particularly from China—pose another key challenge. The research notes that US dependence on China for battery raw materials (BRMs) remains high. A proposed 60% tariff on imports from the world’s largest producer could increase battery cell prices by 29% and raise cathode costs by around $10,000 per tonne.

Taken together, these pressures—higher input costs, reduced subsidies, and policy uncertainty—could push EV prices higher and further slow adoption in the U.S. market.

value.Title: Meta inks 850MW US solar and storage PPAs with DESRI

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK3-D6C3-S8WK-F3FD-00000-00Meta inks 850MW US solar and storage PPAs with DESRI2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:07ZLexisNexisMeta inks 850MW US solar and storage PPAs with DESRI

US technology giant Meta has signed power purchase agreements (PPAs) totalling 850MW with independent power producer (IPP) D.E. Shaw Renewable Investments (DESRI), covering solar and battery storage projects across Oklahoma, Texas and Mississippi.

The agreements, set for 2026, include 500MW in Oklahoma, 200MW in Texas and 150MW in Mississippi. The latest contracts expand the long-running partnership between the two companies, which have now agreed approximately 2,575MW of solar and storage capacity across nine US states to support Metas broader clean energy procurement strategy.

Alongside the 2026 PPAs, the portfolio also includes previously contracted projects such as 150MW in Texas, 300MW in Utah and 100MW in New Mexico, among others.

Our energy infrastructure partnership with Meta now spans nine states and more than two and a half gigawatts across the country, said Hy Martin, chief development officer of DESRI.

DESRI said it expects around 1,110MW of the contracted capacity to begin construction this year, with each project expected to generate hundreds of construction jobs and deliver ongoing local economic benefits over its operational lifetime.

The developer also highlighted community investment provisions embedded within its PPAs, including funding allocations for high school scholarships aimed at students pursuing clean energy careers, with similar initiatives included across multiple states. The companys operating and under-construction renewable energy portfolio comprises more than 80 solar, wind and storage projects, representing over 12GW of total capacity.

Earlier this month, Meta and developer EDP Renewables North America (EDPRNA) signed a PPA for the 250MW Cypress Knee solar project in the US.

Alongside other big tech firms, Meta has made significant investments in buying US solar power in order to fuel its AI and data centre developments. Last month the company signed an agreement to potentially acquire space-based solar power from startup Overview Energy. Its technology claims to be able to use satellites to generate 24/7 solar power in space and beam it back to Earth.

value.Title: ERDC, Cache Energy Advance Long-Duration Energy Storage for Army Installations

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-8RN3-SBJ3-H523-00000-00ERDC, Cache Energy Advance Long-Duration Energy Storage for Army Installations2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:14ZLexisNexisERDC, Cache Energy Advance Long-Duration Energy Storage for Army InstallationsTargeted News ServiceVICKSBURG, Mississippi

The U.S. Army Engineer Research and Development Center issued the following news story:

* * *

ERDC, Cache Energy advance long-duration energy storage for Army installations

By Sarah Clark, public affairs specialist, U.S. Army Engineer Research and Development Center

CHAMPAIGN, Ill. -- Researchers at the U.S. Army Engineer Research and Development Center's Construction Engineering Research Laboratory (ERDC-CERL) are advancing a next-generation energy storage capability designed to strengthen installation resilience during extended power disruptions.

The effort focuses on Thermochemical Energy Storage (TCES), following advancements in materials technology that could provide multiday energy operation, offering a low-cost alternative to diesel backup systems and conventional batteries. As Army installations face growing risks from grid instability, natural disasters and contested logistics environments, this research supports the Department of War's mandate for installations to sustain critical missions during prolonged outages.

To accomplish this effort, the ERDC-CERL team partnered with Cache Energy to accelerate the adaptation of the company's TCES technology for Army use. While Cache Energy has commercial systems operating in the range of 100-1000 kilowatts, this partnership is advancing the design of a specialized 5-kilowatt reactor specifically to provide the Army with a tangible prototype to service multiple applications.

Unlike traditional lithium-ion batteries, which typically provide only a few hours of electricity, the TCES system under development uses calcium oxide-based pellets to store thermal energy through a reversible chemical reaction. When exposed to moisture during discharge, the material releases heat that can later be converted into usable power.

"We're proud to partner with the ERDC-CERL team to demonstrate thermochemical energy storage for military applications," said Amulya Nimmagadda, the director of research and development at Cache Energy. "We are excited by the opportunity to support the U.S. Army's mission by delivering low-cost, long-duration energy storage that enhances resilience, operational readiness and energy security. With multiple installations already underway across diverse applications, we're excited to scale our technology to provide reliable, cost-effective energy solutions for the Army's needs."

The calcium-based material offers several operational advantages. It is low-cost, recyclable, stable in extreme temperatures and capable of high energy density, making it especially promising for military applications in austere or environmentally challenging conditions. Researchers estimate the system capital cost to as low as $5-10 per kilowatt-hour, which is 10x cheaper than existing thermal energy storage, and 100x below current large-scale battery systems.

Beyond reducing diesel fuel demand during winter heating peaks, the system could also work alongside battery storage and renewable energy systems to improve the resilience of contingency bases and fixed installations alike.

The project has progressed rapidly, highlighting ERDC's focus on accelerating promising technologies toward operational use. Thus far, the prototype design has been completed and major system components have been acquired. Looking ahead, the project will transition into prototype assembly, testing and optimization, followed by a live demonstration at ERDC-CERL to showcase the capability in an Army-relevant environment. These efforts position the laboratory to validate this technology as a resilient long-duration energy storage solution for Army installations by FY27.

"This effort reflects ERDC-CERL's commitment to delivering practical, scalable energy solutions that increase installation readiness," said Scott Lux, a member of the ERDC-CERL project team alongside Dominique Gilbert and Natalie Becerra-Stasiewicz. "By advancing thermochemical energy storage, we are providing the Army with a capability that extends operational endurance, reduces logistical risk and supports mission assurance in increasingly complex and contested environments."

By laying the groundwork for scalable, long-duration energy storage, ERDC-CERL's TCES research, in collaboration with Cache Energy, supports the Army's broader goals of energy independence, installation survivability and mission assurance in contested environments.

* * *

urn:contentItem:6JJD-VWS3-RWF8-6168-00000-00Greencarnewsbreaks - Bollinger Innovations, Inc. (NASDAQ: BINI) Cuts Quarterly Cash Burn By 58% While Advancing EV Sales2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:07ZLexisNexisGreencarnewsbreaks - Bollinger Innovations, Inc. (NASDAQ: BINI) Cuts Quarterly Cash Burn By 58% While Advancing EV Sales

Bollinger Innovations (NASDAQ: BINI), an electric vehicle manufacturer, announced it has reduced its quarterly cash burn from $21.2 million to $8.9 million, a 58% decrease. The Company said cost-cutting measures have been implemented without affecting marketing, sales, or manufacturing capabilities as EV sales momentum grows across commercial vehicle Classes 1, 3 and 4. Its lineup includes the Class 1 urban delivery EV cargo van, the Class 3 urban utility EV cab chassis truck, and the Bollinger B4 Chassis Cab, an all-electric Class 4 commercial truck designed with extensive fleet and upfitter input. All vehicles are available for sale in the U.S. and comply with Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards, EPA regulations, and CARB certifications.To view the full press release, visit
About Bollinger Innovations, Inc.


Bollinger Innovations (NASDAQ: BINI) is a SouthernCalifornia-based automotive company building the next generation of commercial electric vehicles (“EVs”) with a U.S. based vehicle manufacturing facility located in Tunica, Mississippi. Both the ONE, a Class 1 EV cargo van, and THREE, a Class 3 EV cab chassis truck, are available for sale in the U.S. The Company's commercial dealer network consists of six dealers, which includes Papé Kenworth, Pritchard EV, National Auto Fleet Group, Ziegler Truck Group, Range Truck Group and Randy Marion Auto Group, providing sales and service coverage in key West Coast, Midwest, Pacific Northwest, New England, and Mid-Atlantic markets. Bollinger Motors, of Oak Park, Michigan, is an established EV truck company of Bollinger Innovations. Bollinger Motors has passed numerous milestones including its B4, Class 4 electric truck production launch on Sept. 16, 2024, and the development of a world-class dealer network with over 50 locations across the United States for sales and service support.
NOTE TO INVESTORS: The latest news and updates relating to BINI are available in the company's newsroom at
About GreenCarStocks
GreenCarStocks (“GCS”) is a specialized communications platform with a focus on electric vehicles (“EVs”) and the green energy sector. It is one of 70+ brands within the Dynamic Brand Portfolio IBN: (1) access to a vast network of wire solutions via InvestorWire to efficiently and effectively reach a myriad of target markets, demographics and diverse industries ; (2) article and editorial syndication to 5,000+ outlets ; (3) enhanced press release enhancement to ensure maximum impact ; (4) social media distribution via IBN to millions of social media followers ; and (5) a full array of tailored corporate communications solutions. With broad reach and a seasoned team of contributing journalists and writers, GCS is uniquely positioned to best serve private and public companies that want to reach a wide audience of investors, influencers, consumers, journalists and the general public. By cutting through the overload of information in today's market, GCS brings its clients unparalleled recognition and brand awareness. GCS is where breaking news, insightful content and actionable information converge.

value.Title: OPINION: The dark side of the data center boom
value.Overview: The South County Spotlight (Scappoose, Oregon), NEWS, 678words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH9-GYY3-S9NN-T0NV-00000-00OPINION: The dark side of the data center boom2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:07ZLexisNexisNEWSOPINION: The dark side of the data center boomLauren Bishop

Across the country, resistance to data centers is rising even as plans are steadily being made to build new ones.

According to the Pew Research Center, a majority of new data centers 67% are being built in rural areas. And three-quarters of those are in Midwestern and Southern towns.

The negative effects have not gone unnoticed. A new data center in Southaven, Mississippi, for example, is reportedly terrorizing the community with high levels of noise and air pollution, and residents are now regretting its existence.

But it's not just the pollution, the depletion of water systems, and the increased energy costs to consumers that should lead communities to resist data centers. When you dig a little deeper, you begin to see how data centers are built on exploitation that goes far beyond small-town USA.

Data centers are both products and producers of wars that kill people and destroy the planet on a global scale. The rapid expansion of these data centers requires raw materials, especially fossil fuels resources often obtained through violence and they fuel a technology that is increasingly used to commit war crimes.

Fossil fuels provide almost 60% of the power for data centers, especially for "emergency generators." AI data centers run almost 24/7, so these "emergency" generators are consistently operating.

Control over fossil fuels, of course, is a driving factor behind the U.S. regime change efforts in Iran, Venezuela and other resource-rich regions. And the extraction of other needed minerals like silicon, gallium, lithium and cobalt requires both the destabilization of the sovereign regions in which they are found and inhumane mining practices, including the use of child labor.

Then there is the question of the moral and ethical use of generative AI. The expansion of data centers comes at a time when AI and LLMs (large language models) are increasingly being used by the Pentagon for militarism domestically and internationally.

The Pentagon recently agreed to massive deals with both Palantir and OpenAI. The employment of AI in military operations has already resulted in war crimes. For instance, Anthropic's Claude was used in the bombing of the girls' school in Minab, Iran, which killed around 170 students and teachers. Do towns that pride themselves on family values want to be behind a killing machine capable of murdering young girls?

It's easy to understand why the announcement of these data centers can seem like good news for areas facing dire economic conditions. Existing low-wage jobs are difficult to survive on. But the evidence suggests data centers create very few local jobs in the towns where they're built. Should this small number of jobs come at the expense of people and the future of our planet?

The state officials brokering these deals with tech companies could instead work on bringing jobs that design, install and maintain renewable energy systems to replace fossil fuel reliance. They could sign contracts with companies that manage and protect the beautiful natural ecosystems, habitats, and biodiversity that often surround rural towns.

We need jobs that sustain the heartbeat of the Midwest and the charm and hospitality of the South not jobs in an industry that terrorizes communities and kills people.

Data centers are not just toxic installations in communities' backyards they are a driving force behind wars and instability, and they keep American workers tied to the endless cycle of wars for fossil fuels.

In defense of the planet, our communities, and communities around the world, I hope urban and rural communities alike can unite to stop data center projects especially across the Midwest and the South, where they have so much beauty and love to protect.

Rural communities' future is not AI. We should be investing in what makes us great: the people and the land.

------------

Melissa Garriga is the communications and media analysis manager for CODEPINK. She was born and raised in Mississippi, where she continues to live and work. This op-ed was distributed by OtherWords.org.

Then there is the question of the moral and ethical use of generative AI. The expansion of data centers comes at a time when AI and LLMs (large language models) are increasingly being used by the Pentagon for militarism domestically and internationally.

The Pentagon recently agreed to massive deals with both Palantir and OpenAI. The employment of AI in military operations has already resulted in war crimes. For instance, Anthropic's Claude was used in the bombing of the girls' school in Minab, Iran, which killed around 170 students and teachers. Do towns that pride themselves on family values want to be behind a killing machine capable of murdering young girls?

It's easy to understand why the announcement of these data centers can seem like good news for areas facing dire economic conditions. Existing low-wage jobs are difficult to survive on. But the evidence suggests data centers create very few local jobs in the towns where they're built. Should this small number of jobs come at the expense of people and the future of our planet?

The state officials brokering these deals with tech companies could instead work on bringing jobs that design, install and maintain renewable energy systems to replace fossil fuel reliance. They could sign contracts with companies that manage and protect the beautiful natural ecosystems, habitats, and biodiversity that often surround rural towns.

We need jobs that sustain the heartbeat of the Midwest and the charm and hospitality of the South not jobs in an industry that terrorizes communities and kills people.

Data centers are not just toxic installations in communities' backyards they are a driving force behind wars and instability, and they keep American workers tied to the endless cycle of wars for fossil fuels.

In defense of the planet, our communities, and communities around the world, I hope urban and rural communities alike can unite to stop data center projects especially across the Midwest and the South, where they have so much beauty and love to protect.

Rural communities' future is not AI. We should be investing in what makes us great: the people and the land.

------------

Melissa Garriga is the communications and media analysis manager for CODEPINK. She was born and raised in Mississippi, where she continues to live and work. This op-ed was distributed by OtherWords.org.

value.Title: OPINION: The dark side of the data center boom

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH9-GYY3-S9GF-H54R-00000-00OPINION: The dark side of the data center boom2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:07ZLexisNexisNEWSOPINION: The dark side of the data center boomLauren Bishop

Across the country, resistance to data centers is rising even as plans are steadily being made to build new ones.

According to the Pew Research Center, a majority of new data centers 67% are being built in rural areas. And three-quarters of those are in Midwestern and Southern towns.

The negative effects have not gone unnoticed. A new data center in Southaven, Mississippi, for example, is reportedly terrorizing the community with high levels of noise and air pollution, and residents are now regretting its existence.

But it's not just the pollution, the depletion of water systems, and the increased energy costs to consumers that should lead communities to resist data centers. When you dig a little deeper, you begin to see how data centers are built on exploitation that goes far beyond small-town USA.

Data centers are both products and producers of wars that kill people and destroy the planet on a global scale. The rapid expansion of these data centers requires raw materials, especially fossil fuels resources often obtained through violence and they fuel a technology that is increasingly used to commit war crimes.

Fossil fuels provide almost 60% of the power for data centers, especially for "emergency generators." AI data centers run almost 24/7, so these "emergency" generators are consistently operating.

Control over fossil fuels, of course, is a driving factor behind the U.S. regime change efforts in Iran, Venezuela and other resource-rich regions. And the extraction of other needed minerals like silicon, gallium, lithium and cobalt requires both the destabilization of the sovereign regions in which they are found and inhumane mining practices, including the use of child labor.

Then there is the question of the moral and ethical use of generative AI. The expansion of data centers comes at a time when AI and LLMs (large language models) are increasingly being used by the Pentagon for militarism domestically and internationally.

The Pentagon recently agreed to massive deals with both Palantir and OpenAI. The employment of AI in military operations has already resulted in war crimes. For instance, Anthropic's Claude was used in the bombing of the girls' school in Minab, Iran, which killed around 170 students and teachers. Do towns that pride themselves on family values want to be behind a killing machine capable of murdering young girls?

It's easy to understand why the announcement of these data centers can seem like good news for areas facing dire economic conditions. Existing low-wage jobs are difficult to survive on. But the evidence suggests data centers create very few local jobs in the towns where they're built. Should this small number of jobs come at the expense of people and the future of our planet?

The state officials brokering these deals with tech companies could instead work on bringing jobs that design, install and maintain renewable energy systems to replace fossil fuel reliance. They could sign contracts with companies that manage and protect the beautiful natural ecosystems, habitats, and biodiversity that often surround rural towns.

We need jobs that sustain the heartbeat of the Midwest and the charm and hospitality of the South not jobs in an industry that terrorizes communities and kills people.

Data centers are not just toxic installations in communities' backyards they are a driving force behind wars and instability, and they keep American workers tied to the endless cycle of wars for fossil fuels.

In defense of the planet, our communities, and communities around the world, I hope urban and rural communities alike can unite to stop data center projects especially across the Midwest and the South, where they have so much beauty and love to protect.

Rural communities' future is not AI. We should be investing in what makes us great: the people and the land.

------------

Melissa Garriga is the communications and media analysis manager for CODEPINK. She was born and raised in Mississippi, where she continues to live and work. This op-ed was distributed by OtherWords.org.

value.Title: OPINION: The dark side of the data center boom

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH9-GYY3-S9GF-H4K3-00000-00OPINION: The dark side of the data center boom2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:07ZLexisNexisNEWSOPINION: The dark side of the data center boomLauren Bishop

Across the country, resistance to data centers is rising even as plans are steadily being made to build new ones.

According to the Pew Research Center, a majority of new data centers 67% are being built in rural areas. And three-quarters of those are in Midwestern and Southern towns.

The negative effects have not gone unnoticed. A new data center in Southaven, Mississippi, for example, is reportedly terrorizing the community with high levels of noise and air pollution, and residents are now regretting its existence.

But it's not just the pollution, the depletion of water systems, and the increased energy costs to consumers that should lead communities to resist data centers. When you dig a little deeper, you begin to see how data centers are built on exploitation that goes far beyond small-town USA.

Data centers are both products and producers of wars that kill people and destroy the planet on a global scale. The rapid expansion of these data centers requires raw materials, especially fossil fuels resources often obtained through violence and they fuel a technology that is increasingly used to commit war crimes.

Fossil fuels provide almost 60% of the power for data centers, especially for "emergency generators." AI data centers run almost 24/7, so these "emergency" generators are consistently operating.

Control over fossil fuels, of course, is a driving factor behind the U.S. regime change efforts in Iran, Venezuela and other resource-rich regions. And the extraction of other needed minerals like silicon, gallium, lithium and cobalt requires both the destabilization of the sovereign regions in which they are found and inhumane mining practices, including the use of child labor.

Then there is the question of the moral and ethical use of generative AI. The expansion of data centers comes at a time when AI and LLMs (large language models) are increasingly being used by the Pentagon for militarism domestically and internationally.

The Pentagon recently agreed to massive deals with both Palantir and OpenAI. The employment of AI in military operations has already resulted in war crimes. For instance, Anthropic's Claude was used in the bombing of the girls' school in Minab, Iran, which killed around 170 students and teachers. Do towns that pride themselves on family values want to be behind a killing machine capable of murdering young girls?

It's easy to understand why the announcement of these data centers can seem like good news for areas facing dire economic conditions. Existing low-wage jobs are difficult to survive on. But the evidence suggests data centers create very few local jobs in the towns where they're built. Should this small number of jobs come at the expense of people and the future of our planet?

The state officials brokering these deals with tech companies could instead work on bringing jobs that design, install and maintain renewable energy systems to replace fossil fuel reliance. They could sign contracts with companies that manage and protect the beautiful natural ecosystems, habitats, and biodiversity that often surround rural towns.

We need jobs that sustain the heartbeat of the Midwest and the charm and hospitality of the South not jobs in an industry that terrorizes communities and kills people.

Data centers are not just toxic installations in communities' backyards they are a driving force behind wars and instability, and they keep American workers tied to the endless cycle of wars for fossil fuels.

In defense of the planet, our communities, and communities around the world, I hope urban and rural communities alike can unite to stop data center projects especially across the Midwest and the South, where they have so much beauty and love to protect.

Rural communities' future is not AI. We should be investing in what makes us great: the people and the land.

------------

Melissa Garriga is the communications and media analysis manager for CODEPINK. She was born and raised in Mississippi, where she continues to live and work. This op-ed was distributed by OtherWords.org.

value.Title: Entergy New Orleans uses drone technology to strengthen reliability

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH9-NWV3-SBT2-54KS-00000-00Entergy New Orleans uses drone technology to strengthen reliability2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:07ZLexisNexisEntergy New Orleans uses drone technology to strengthen reliabilityTargeted News ServiceNEW ORLEANS, Louisiana

[Category: BizEnergy] -- Entergy posted the following news release:

* * *

Entergy New Orleans uses drone technology to strengthen reliability

*

Entergy New Orleans is committed to enhancing grid reliability and improving the quality of service for our customers. Since 2019, the company has deployed advanced drone technology to inspect transmission lines spanning critical infrastructure and at challenging locations. At present, Entergy New Orleans is the only operating company within the enterprise utilizing this technology for inspection purposes.ÂÂ

In 2017, a lightning strike near the I-10 and I-610 interchange damaged a utility pole, resulting in live wires falling onto the interstate. This incident led to significant traffic delays and a widespread power outage, highlighting the importance of safer and more proactive ways to monitor critical infrastructure.

"Using drone technology gives us the ability to safely inspect infrastructure that can be challenging to access through traditional methods," said a Jason Goodick, manager of corporate aviation at Entergy. "It allows our engineers to review detailed imagery, identify potential issues early, and make proactive repairs that help improve reliability for our customers."

Federal regulations restrict drone operations over critical infrastructure, and industry guidelines recommend maintaining a minimum distance of 35 feet from active transmission lines. To ensure safe and compliant inspections, we employ specialized flight protocols and leverage advanced technology.

Our drones capture high-resolution video of transmission lines and equipment, along with infrared imagery that can reveal potential issues that are not visible during ground inspections. This footage is then reviewed by our engineering teams, enabling us to prioritize maintenance and address concerns before they potentially lead to outages.

Through routine inspections prior to storm season and throughout the year, we are strengthening safety protocols, minimizing service interruptions, and implementing innovative strategies to ensure the reliable maintenance of the electric grid.

Click here to watch the drone video footage.

About Entergy New Orleans

Entergy New Orleans provides electricity to more than 209,000 customers in Orleans Parish, Louisiana. Entergy New Orleans is a subsidiary of Entergy Corporation. Entergy generates, transmits and distributes electricity to power life for more than 3 million customers through our operating companies in Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi and Texas. We're focused on keeping costs for our customers as low as possible while providing reliable energy that our communities count on. We're also investing in growth for the future with a more resilient, cleaner energy system that includes modern natural gas, nuclear and renewable energy generation. As a nationally recognized leader in sustainability and corporate citizenship, we deliver more than $100 million in economic benefits each year to the communities we serve through philanthropy, volunteerism and advocacy. Entergy is a Fortune 500 company headquartered in New Orleans, Louisiana, and has approximately 12,000 employees. Learn more at EntergyNewOrleans.com and connect with @EntergyNOLA on social media.

***

Past Month: Monthly: Entergy Corporation (NYSE:ETR) decreases 3% on robust volume

Entergy Corporation (NYSE:ETR $112.90), the NYSE's 4th largest Conventional Electricity company by market cap, has decreased $2.94 (or 2.5%) in the past month to close at $112.90 on Thursday May 14, ending a four-month streak of rises. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 533.9 points (or 7.7%) in the month, this represented a relative price change of -10.2%.

The volume was 1.4 times average trading of 60.2 million shares.

Activities

Entergy Corporation is a major U.S.-based energy provider listed on the New York Stock Exchange, delivering reliable electric power to over 3 million customers across Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Texas. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, distribution, and sale of electricity through a diversified portfolio that includes nuclear, natural gas, coal, and renewable sources. In recent years, Entergy has prioritized growth in renewable energy segments, particularly solar and wind projects, which have expanded rapidly due to increasing demand for clean power and supportive regulatory environments. These initiatives align with the company's broader strategy to enhance grid reliability, reduce carbon emissions, and integrate advanced technologies for efficient energy delivery, positioning Entergy as a key player in the evolving utility sector. It is the NYSE's 4th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

Entergy Corporation (NYSE:ETR) stands as a pivotal player in the energy sector, underscoring its influence with the Entergy Texas Orange County Advanced Power Station. Achieving the Entergy Orange County first fire marks a crucial EntergyTexas first fire milestone. With the Orange County Advanced Power Station first fire accomplished, the subsequent Orange County power station commissioning progresses smoothly. This Entergy power station start-up highlights Entergy advanced power station capabilities. Noteworthy is the new Entergy power plant Orange County, vital for grid reliability EntergyTexas. As Entergy Texas power generation capacity expands, the first fire milestone power plant enhances Entergy utility infrastructure. Through Entergy plant commissioning timeline and operational readiness testing, commercial operation Entergy advances. These Entergy energy infrastructure projects epitomize industrial power solutions Entergy. Power station milestone news Entergy underscores Entergy NYSE:ETR power projects while bolstering Entergy Texas energy reliability. These initiatives bolster Entergy community energy projects and Entergy electricity supply solutions, fortifying their market position.

Activities

Deckers Outdoor Corporation, listed on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE: DECK), is a global leader in designing, marketing, and distributing innovative footwear, apparel, and accessories. The company's portfolio includes popular brands such as UGG, Hoka, Teva, and Sanuk, focusing on casual, outdoor, and performance products that emphasize comfort, quality, and style. Core activities involve product development, global distribution through wholesale and direct-to-consumer channels, and strategic brand management to drive consumer engagement. In recent years, the fastest-growing segments have been Hoka, which has surged due to rising demand for high-performance running and athletic footwear, and UGG's expansion into lifestyle apparel. This growth has bolstered Deckers' revenue, enabling investments in sustainability and digital initiatives to strengthen its market position in the competitive outdoor and athletic goods sector. It is the NYSE's 7th largest Consumer cyclical company by market capitalisation.

Deckers Outdoor Corporation (NYSE:DECK) is a global leader in designing, marketing, and distributing innovative footwear, with a diverse portfolio that includes UGG, HOKA, Teva, and Sanuk. Deckers excels in creative collaborations, exemplified by the UGG x PinkPantheress collection, featuring UGG PinkPantheress boots and the UGG limited edition PinkPantheress line. The UGG Classic Mini PinkPantheress and UGG platform slippers are at the forefront of UGG festival footwear, appealing to style-conscious consumers. In addition, Deckers’ HOKA running shoes, such as the HOKA Clifton running shoes and HOKA Bondi sneakers, are renowned for their superior performance and comfort. Teva festival sandals and Sanuk casual sandals further diversify its product offerings. With a robust supply chain and strong brand recognition, Deckers is strategically positioned for sustained growth and resilience in the competitive footwear market, capitalizing on consumer trends and UGG celebrity collaboration collection initiatives.

Past Month: Monthly: Entergy Corporation (NYSE:ETR) decreases 3% on robust volume

Entergy Corporation (NYSE:ETR $112.93), the NYSE's 4th largest Conventional Electricity company by market cap, has decreased $2.91 (or 2.5%) in the past month to close at $112.93 on Tuesday May 12, ending a three-month streak of rises. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 584.1 points (or 8.6%) in the month, this represented a relative price change of -11.1%.

The volume was 1.4 times average trading of 60.4 million shares.

Activities

Entergy Corporation is a major U.S.-based energy provider listed on the New York Stock Exchange, delivering reliable electric power to over 3 million customers across Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Texas. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, distribution, and sale of electricity through a diversified portfolio that includes nuclear, natural gas, coal, and renewable sources. In recent years, Entergy has prioritized growth in renewable energy segments, particularly solar and wind projects, which have expanded rapidly due to increasing demand for clean power and supportive regulatory environments. These initiatives align with the company's broader strategy to enhance grid reliability, reduce carbon emissions, and integrate advanced technologies for efficient energy delivery, positioning Entergy as a key player in the evolving utility sector. It is the NYSE's 4th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

Entergy Corporation (NYSE:ETR) stands as a pivotal player in the energy sector, underscoring its influence with the Entergy Texas Orange County Advanced Power Station. Achieving the Entergy Orange County first fire marks a crucial EntergyTexas first fire milestone. With the Orange County Advanced Power Station first fire accomplished, the subsequent Orange County power station commissioning progresses smoothly. This Entergy power station start-up highlights Entergy advanced power station capabilities. Noteworthy is the new Entergy power plant Orange County, vital for grid reliability EntergyTexas. As Entergy Texas power generation capacity expands, the first fire milestone power plant enhances Entergy utility infrastructure. Through Entergy plant commissioning timeline and operational readiness testing, commercial operation Entergy advances. These Entergy energy infrastructure projects epitomize industrial power solutions Entergy. Power station milestone news Entergy underscores Entergy NYSE:ETR power projects while bolstering Entergy Texas energy reliability. These initiatives bolster Entergy community energy projects and Entergy electricity supply solutions, fortifying their market position.

Past Month: Monthly: Deckers Outdoor Corporation (NYSE:DECK) sinks 12% on weak volume

Deckers Outdoor Corporation (NYSE:DECK $94.89), the NYSE's 9th largest Consumer Cyclical company by market cap, has sunk $12.97 (or 12.0%) in the past month to close at $94.89 on Tuesday May 12. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 584.1 points (or 8.6%) in the month, this represented a relative price change of -20.6%.

The volume was 0.6 times average trading of 65.7 million shares.

Activities

Deckers Outdoor Corporation, listed on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE: DECK), is a global leader in designing, marketing, and distributing innovative footwear, apparel, and accessories. The company's portfolio includes popular brands such as UGG, Hoka, Teva, and Sanuk, focusing on casual, outdoor, and performance products that emphasize comfort, quality, and style. Core activities involve product development, global distribution through wholesale and direct-to-consumer channels, and strategic brand management to drive consumer engagement. In recent years, the fastest-growing segments have been Hoka, which has surged due to rising demand for high-performance running and athletic footwear, and UGG's expansion into lifestyle apparel. This growth has bolstered Deckers' revenue, enabling investments in sustainability and digital initiatives to strengthen its market position in the competitive outdoor and athletic goods sector. It is the NYSE's 7th largest Consumer cyclical company by market capitalisation.

Deckers Outdoor Corporation (NYSE:DECK) is a global leader in designing, marketing, and distributing innovative footwear, with a diverse portfolio that includes UGG, HOKA, Teva, and Sanuk. Deckers excels in creative collaborations, exemplified by the UGG x PinkPantheress collection, featuring UGG PinkPantheress boots and the UGG limited edition PinkPantheress line. The UGG Classic Mini PinkPantheress and UGG platform slippers are at the forefront of UGG festival footwear, appealing to style-conscious consumers. In addition, Deckers’ HOKA running shoes, such as the HOKA Clifton running shoes and HOKA Bondi sneakers, are renowned for their superior performance and comfort. Teva festival sandals and Sanuk casual sandals further diversify its product offerings. With a robust supply chain and strong brand recognition, Deckers is strategically positioned for sustained growth and resilience in the competitive footwear market, capitalizing on consumer trends and UGG celebrity collaboration collection initiatives.

Activities

M&T Bank Corporation is a prominent regional bank holding company listed on the New York Stock Exchange, headquartered in Buffalo, New York, and operating primarily in the Mid-Atlantic and Northeastern United States. Its core business activities include commercial banking, retail banking, and wealth management services, such as lending, deposit products, and investment advisory for individuals, businesses, and institutions. The bank has seen robust growth in its commercial lending and digital banking segments, fueled by strategic acquisitions like the 2022 merger with People's United Bank, which expanded its footprint and enhanced technological capabilities. These areas have driven increased revenue from business loans and online services, supporting overall profitability and market expansion in a competitive financial sector. M&T Bank's focus on innovation and customer-centric strategies positions it as a reliable partner for diverse financial needs. It is the NYSE's 9th largest Bank by market capitalisation.

M&T Bank Corporation (NYSE:MTB) is strategically positioned in the financial industry as a prominent regional bank. The company recently announced an initiative encapsulated by the M&T Bank subordinated notes offering. As part of this strategy, the M&T Bank $500 million subordinated notes issuance signifies a targeted approach towards strengthening its financial position. This MTB subordinated notes offering is a pivotal M&T Bank subordinated debt offering that reflects its commitment to maintaining capital adequacy and supporting growth. The M&T Bank notes offering reported by Investing.com highlights the M&T Bank $500M debt issuance details. The NYSE:MTB subordinated notes are a key component of the M&T Bank bond offering, ensuring robust participation in the M&T Bank debt securities offering and expanding its investor base. Furthermore, M&T Bank's fixed-income offering, including the M&T Bank capital raise subordinated notes, emphasizes its M&T Bank corporate subordinated bonds drive. As noted in the M&T Bank private placement subordinated notes and Investing.com M&T Bank subordinated notes, the M&T Bank investor news subordinated notes strategy underscores the significance of the MTB $500M offering in ongoing M&T Bank corporate debt issuance efforts. This M&T Bank capital markets offering, articulated in the Bank subordinated notes offering, contributes to M&T Bank’s standing in the financial sector. The Regional bank subordinated notes and Bank-issued subordinated notes underline M&T Bank’s broader M&T Bank financing activity subordinated notes framework. The M&T Bank securities offering, highlighted during the MTB notes offering press release, reaffirms the bank's robust M&T Bank debt market offering.

As a global leader in the industry, the company focuses on innovative machinery that enhances productivity and efficiency, including tractors, combines, excavators, and loaders. Its core activities encompass manufacturing high-quality equipment, providing financial services for equipment financing, and offering digital solutions to support modern farming and construction operations. Among its fastest-growing segments are precision agriculture technologies, such as autonomous tractors and data analytics tools, which leverage AI and IoT for optimized resource use, as well as the construction equipment division, driven by increasing global infrastructure projects and demand for sustainable machinery. This strategic emphasis on innovation and expansion positions Deere to meet evolving market needs while maintaining a strong commitment to environmental stewardship. It is the NYSE's largest Industrial company by market capitalisation.

Deere & Company (NYSE:DE) positions itself as an industry leader through its commitment to sustainability and innovation. The Deere & Company recycled plastics initiative demonstrates its efforts towards a circular economy agriculture model. John Deere tractors recycled plastics extend through the use of John Deere composite plastic parts, while John Deere recycled ABS parts signify advancements in material usage. In line with sustainable materials R&D, John Deere plastic recycling in equipment manufacturing is pivotal. Products like John Deere recycled plastic seats, John Deere recycled plastic mower decks, and John Deere recycled plastic cab interiors act as examples of eco-friendly agricultural implements. John Deere plastic component durability testing ensures the longevity of these innovations. Moreover, collaborations with John Deere sustainable parts suppliers enhance the availability of John Deere biodegradable plastic alternatives. John Deere eco-friendly tractors and John Deere green farming equipment are testaments to Deere & Company's leadership in the sustainable farm equipment domain. John Deere tests recycled plastics for quality assurance, manifesting its prowess in recycled plastics prototypes.

Activities

Freeport-McMoRan Inc. is a leading U.S.-based mining company specializing in the exploration, development, and production of copper, gold, and molybdenum. Headquartered in Phoenix, Arizona, the company operates large-scale mines in the Americas and Indonesia, leveraging advanced technologies to extract and process these essential metals for global industries such as electronics, construction, and renewable energy. Its core business focuses on sustainable resource management, emphasizing operational efficiency and environmental stewardship to meet rising demand. The fastest-growing segments include copper production, which has expanded rapidly due to increasing needs in electric vehicles and infrastructure projects, as well as gold operations that have benefited from higher prices and new mine developments. Overall, Freeport-McMoRan's strategic investments in high-potential assets position it as a key player in the global metals market, driving long-term value through diversified revenue streams and innovation. Freeport-McMoRan Inc. (NYSE:FCX) is a leading copper mining company and major copper producer Freeport, known for its extensive operations in Freeport copper production. With distinguished assets such as the Grasberg copper mine, Indonesia copper mines Freeport, and North American copper mines Freeport, the company plays a pivotal role in the global industrial copper supply FCX. Activities

Entergy Corporation is a major U.S.-based energy provider listed on the New York Stock Exchange, delivering reliable electric power to over 3 million customers across Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Texas. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, distribution, and sale of electricity through a diversified portfolio that includes nuclear, natural gas, coal, and renewable sources. In recent years, Entergy has prioritized growth in renewable energy segments, particularly solar and wind projects, which have expanded rapidly due to increasing demand for clean power and supportive regulatory environments. These initiatives align with the company's broader strategy to enhance grid reliability, reduce carbon emissions, and integrate advanced technologies for efficient energy delivery, positioning Entergy as a key player in the evolving utility sector. It is the NYSE's 4th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

Citizens Financial Group, Inc., a leading U.S. bank holding company traded on the New York Stock Exchange, specializes in retail and commercial banking, wealth management, and mortgage lending services. The fastest-growing segments are digital banking platforms, which have expanded rapidly due to increasing consumer demand for mobile and online services, and commercial lending, particularly in small and medium-sized enterprises, fueled by economic recovery and strategic partnerships. With a strong commitment to community banking and sustainable practices, Citizens Financial Group continues to solidify its position as a reliable financial partner in a competitive market. The company's fastest-growing segments include NGL fractionation and logistics, fueled by surging shale production and increasing demand for refined products such as ethane and propane. This growth is further bolstered by strategic investments in infrastructure and acquisitions, enhancing ONEOK's ability to capitalize on the expanding natural gas market while maintaining operational efficiency and environmental stewardship. It is the NYSE's largest Gas distribution company by market capitalisation.

ONEOK, Inc. (NYSE:OKE) provides a robust portfolio of midstream services with a focus on ONEOK NGLs and ONEOK natural gas liquids. Strategically positioned, ONEOK stock (OKE) reflects its potential with sustainable ONEOK fee-based revenue and an impressive ONEOK dividend yield. The ONEOK capex plan supports a comprehensive ONEOK growth backlog, enhancing ONEOK pipeline capacity and ONEOK throughput volumes. Key assets include ONEOK NGL pipelines, ONEOK gas gathering, and ONEOK gas processing plants, optimizing ONEOK operational synergies. ONEOK earnings benefit from ONEOK synergies achieved through focusing on ONEOK NGL fractionation capacity, ONEOK NGL storage, and ONEOK NGL marketing, with specific emphasis on ONEOK propane, ONEOK ethane, ONEOK butane, and ONEOK condensate. With an effective ONEOK FCF forecast, ONEOK free cash flow aids ONEOK debt reduction. The ONEOK distribution coverage ratio is robust due to ONEOK growth projects backlog and ONEOK transportation services. Additionally, ONEOK merger synergies complete this strategic framework.

urn:contentItem:6JGH-8453-S3MR-41JK-00000-00Quarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Entergy reports 63% sequential rise in Quarterly Net Profit Corporate Wire Date: 30 April 2026 10:40 EDT Release Date: April 29, 20262026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:07ZLexisNexisQuarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Entergy reports 63% sequential rise in Quarterly Net Profit Corporate Wire Date: 30 April 2026 10:40 EDT Release Date: April 29, 2026

AMERICAN RESULTS

Entergy (NYSE:ETR), announced net profit of $385m for the quarter-ended 31 March 2026 [Q1/2026], up 63% from the previous quarter [Q4/2025] and up 6.7% from the year-earlier period [Q1/2025]. Earnings per share (EPS) were up 61.5% sequentially from 52.0c in Q4/2025 to 84.0c in Q1/2026.

Major changes compared with previous corresponding period [Mar 31, 2026 vs Mar 31, 2025]:

Favourable Changes:

- Net profit was up 6.7% from $360.8m to $384.9m

- Total revenue was up 12% from $2.8b to $3.2b

Unfavourable Changes:

- EBITDA to Revenues was down from 34.3% to 32%

- Profit before tax to Revenues was down from 16.2% to 15%

- EBIT to Revenues was down from 16.2% to 15%

- Profit after tax to Revenues was down from 12.7% to 12.1%

Note:

- EPS was steady at 84.0c.</dt>

- Depreciation to Revenues was down from 18% to 16.9%

ACTIVITIES:

Entergy Corporation is a major U.S.-based energy provider listed on the New York Stock Exchange, delivering reliable electric power to over 3 million customers across Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Texas. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, distribution, and sale of electricity through a diversified portfolio that includes nuclear, natural gas, coal, and renewable sources. In recent years, Entergy has prioritized growth in renewable energy segments, particularly solar and wind projects, which have expanded rapidly due to increasing demand for clean power and supportive regulatory environments. These initiatives align with the company's broader strategy to enhance grid reliability, reduce carbon emissions, and integrate advanced technologies for efficient energy delivery, positioning Entergy as a key player in the evolving utility sector.

MARKET POSITION:

Entergy Corporation (NYSE:ETR), the NYSE's 4th largest Conventional Electricity company by market cap, jumped $22.84 (or 24.9%) year-to-date (YTD) in 2026 to close at $114.67. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which has risen 4.2% YTD, this is a relative price increase of 20.6%. A zero-bagger in 2025, the value of USD1,000 invested two years ago is $2,445 [vs $1,402 for the S&P 500 Index], for a capital gain of $1,293 and dividend reinvested of $152. The average annualised return to shareholders has been 56.4% in the past two years. The average daily volume of 2.4 million shares so far in 2026 was 0.8 times the average daily volume in 2025.

value.Title: Southern Company Q1 2026 Operational Highlights and Key Risks *Updated*

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-KSM3-S0XP-40MH-00000-00Southern Company Q1 2026 Operational Highlights and Key Risks *Updated*2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:07ZLexisNexisSouthern Company Q1 2026 Operational Highlights and Key Risks *Updated*

NYSE STOCK REPORT

This article includes relevant portions of a research report that we wrote and published analyzing Southern Company's Q1 2026 10-Q filing, which was made available following the company's earnings release on April 30, 2026.

INDEX

SECTION 1 SECTION 1 Primary Risk Factors Analysis

SECTION 2 Financials as reported (Quarter ending 31 March 2026)

SECTION 1 SECTION 1 Primary Risk Factors Analysis

Regulatory and Legislative Changes

- Potential Impact: Evolving federal and state legislative frameworks, particularly stringent environmental regulations governing coal combustion residuals (CCR), shifts in corporate tax policy, and modifications to established rate recovery mechanisms, pose a foundational structural risk to the fundamental cost frameworks and forward-looking capital deployment strategies of the enterprise. The operational entities face materially increased compliance expenditures directly associated with ongoing and future CCR management and asset retirement obligations. Furthermore, potential legislative alterations or restrictive interpretations concerning production tax credits (PTCs) and investment tax credits (ITCs) under the statutory frameworks of the OBBB could severely limit the eligibility of the enterprise for tax-advantaged financing on renewable energy projects, particularly those projects whose supply chains or capital structures are influenced by foreign entities of concern. On the state level, ongoing and upcoming rate cases alongside regulatory proceedings adjudicated by Public Service Commissions (PSCs) in Alabama, Georgia, and Mississippi possess a direct, material influence over the organization's overarching ability to successfully and timely recover substantial capital expenditures. These capital outflows are primarily allocated toward new generation capacity, comprehensive transmission grid modernization, and essential storm damage restoration efforts. Any administrative delay or regulatory denial in the subsequent rate recovery for these large-scale capital investments-particularly those necessitated by the rapid load growth driven by the expansion of hyperscale data centers-possesses the potential to severely compress authorized returns on equity (ROE) and strain consolidated operating cash flows. Additionally, statutory or regulatory restrictions imposed on base rate increases, such as the mandated restrictions specifically governing operations within the state of Alabama through the year 2028, mechanically limit the enterprise's pricing flexibility and revenue realization amidst an environment characterized by broadly rising macroeconomic and operational costs.

- Investor Assessment: Institutional investors and fixed-income allocators must rigorously monitor ongoing state PSC dockets alongside evolving federal guidance from the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the Department of the Treasury. The pervasive regulatory lag associated with cost recovery mechanisms represents a core earnings and cash flow risk, particularly given the heavy construction and capital expenditure cycle currently underway across the operating footprint. This specific risk vector is distinctly heightened by the capital-intensive nature inherently required to meet projected geometric demand growth while simultaneously attempting to maintain investment-grade credit metrics and balance sheet health.

Construction and Project Execution Risks

- Potential Impact: The Southern Company system is presently navigating a period of significantly elevated capital spending concentrated on expanding generation capacity-including natural gas turbines, utility-scale battery storage installations, and nuclear facility lifecycle extensions-as well as comprehensive transmission and distribution network upgrades. Unforeseen schedule delays, systemic cost overruns, or a failure to successfully obtain necessary environmental permits and regulatory certifications in a timely manner could materially inflate Construction Work in Progress (CWIP) asset balances and correspondingly elevate carrying and financing costs. Specific, identifiable execution risks natively include ongoing macroeconomic supply chain disruptions, skilled labor shortages within specialized construction trades, adverse severe weather events impacting project sites, judicial or regulatory challenges levied by adversarial parties against approved projects, and complex systemic integration issues upon the commissioning of new generation units. Furthermore, Southern Power's active portfolio of wind repowering initiatives and greenfield solar projects inherently carries substantial accelerated depreciation exposure until the respective assets reach their formal Commercial Operation Date (COD). The integration of large load contracts, primarily driven by the proliferation of regional data centers, additionally introduces acute revenue recovery uncertainty in scenarios where the actual megawatt load ramps at a fundamentally slower velocity than initially projected in the underlying offtake models.

- Investor Assessment: The current operational profile dictates that this is a highly execution-heavy risk period for the enterprise. Investors are advised to actively track the accumulation of Allowance for Funds Used During Construction (AFUDC) trends, the velocity of CWIP balance conversions to in-service utility plant, and the cadence of administrative regulatory approvals corresponding to major capital projects, such as Georgia Power's Integrated Resource Plan (IRP) resource allocations. Persistent, unmitigated cost overruns across the construction portfolio possess the capacity to exert downward pressure on consolidated credit ratings and challenge long-term dividend payout sustainability.

Fuel Price and Supply Volatility

- Potential Impact: Material fluctuations in the prevailing market prices for natural gas, thermal coal, and wholesale purchased power directly and immediately affect aggregate operating expenses across the portfolio of traditional regulated utilities. While established statutory fuel recovery clauses and pass-through mechanisms generally provide for the ultimate recovery of these commodity costs, the structural timing mismatches between expense realization and rate recovery, coupled with mandatory regulatory prudence reviews, systematically create episodic cash flow friction and observable earnings variability. Southern Company Gas explicitly faces direct commodity price exposure and basis risk across its gas marketing services and commercial storage operations. Furthermore, structurally higher underlying commodity prices can adversely impact aggregate customer affordability metrics, thereby introducing demand elasticity risks and potentially elevating bad debt expense allocations.

- Mitigation Strategies: Fuel recovery mechanisms and contractual pass-through frameworks are actively utilized to mitigate direct exposure to underlying commodity price volatility.

- Investor Assessment: While established fuel recovery mechanisms insulate the core earnings profile, they do not entirely eliminate working capital friction or overarching regulatory risk. Investors must diligently monitor wholesale natural gas price volatility and its downstream translation into aggregate customer retail bills, as a prolonged period of elevated energy prices possesses the potential to either accelerate structural consumer electrification trends or catalyze adverse regulatory and political pushback regarding affordability.

Demand Growth and Large Load Uncertainty

- Potential Impact: The enterprise is projecting significant forward electricity demand growth, catalyzed primarily by the rapid regional deployment of hyperscale data centers and broader industrial electrification initiatives. This demand trajectory necessitates the immediate, substantial deployment of new capital toward both generation capacity and transmission infrastructure. Associated risks primarily manifest as a slower-than-expected customer facility ramp-up, premature offtake contract terminations, or a structural failure to adequately recover the incremental infrastructural costs through approved rate designs. This dynamic effectively creates acute capital access constraints and Return on Equity (ROE) pressure in the event that the projected load fails to materialize exactly as contractually forecasted.

- Mitigation Strategies: The executed large load contracts are structured to include various downside risk protections, including mandatory minimum billing thresholds and upfront capital contributions from the counterparties.

- Investor Assessment: This dynamic represents a bifurcated vector of both immense growth opportunity and substantial operational risk. While downside protections are structurally embedded within the counterparties' agreements, the tangible execution risk remains elevated. Financial modeling should continuously monitor the variance between actual megawatt load additions and the originally contracted volume estimates, directly cross-referencing these figures against approved rate base growth trajectories.

Cybersecurity and Physical Security Threats

- Potential Impact: The threat landscape encompasses potential sophisticated cyber intrusions or coordinated physical attacks targeting the regional electric grid, baseload and peaking generation facilities, or critical natural gas transmission infrastructure. A successful breach or physical disruption could directly precipitate widespread operational disruptions, critical safety incidents, sensitive internal or customer data breaches, and the subsequent imposition of severe regulatory penalties. The compounding digitization of core utility operations, alongside an increasing structural reliance on decentralized third-party software and service vendors, materially heightens the aggregate attack surface and operational exposure.

- Investor Assessment: This exposure categorizes as an enterprise-wide systemic risk possessing the distinct potential to cause both significant, prolonged service outages and permanent reputational degradation. The investor base should analytically evaluate the enterprise's ongoing capital allocation toward grid modernization, digital hardening, and cybersecurity infrastructure as a foundational, core resiliency metric governing long-term asset security.

Interest Rate and Financing Risks

- Potential Impact: An environment characterized by rising nominal interest rates inherently increases the prevailing cost of new debt issuance and directly impacts the calculation of AFUDC credits across the construction portfolio. Structurally higher financing costs required to fund the expansive, multi-year large capital deployment program possess the capacity to exert mechanical pressure on target credit metrics and ultimately elevate customer retail rates. Additionally, the mandatory refinancing of existing maturing debt tranches at higher prevailing market yields mathematically adds to the ongoing interest expense burden, directly impacting the bottom line.

- Investor Assessment: Given the presence of significant outstanding long-term debt liabilities combined with substantial ongoing external borrowing requirements, the enterprise's exposure to interest rate volatility is considered highly material. Analysts must continuously track Funds from Operations to Total Debt (FFO/Debt) ratios alongside the spread between the regulatory allowed ROEs and the enterprise's incrementally rising weighted average cost of capital (WACC).

Environmental and Climate-Related Risks

- Potential Impact: The implementation of increasingly strict CCR management rules, the potential introduction of punitive carbon pricing or regulatory emissions caps, and the direct physical risks stemming from extreme weather events-specifically coastal hurricanes and severe inland storms-possess the capability to drive materially higher ongoing compliance expenditures and acute post-storm infrastructure restoration costs. Transition risks encompass the financial and operational friction associated with mandated systemic shifts away from traditional natural gas and coal-fired generation profiles, particularly if legislative policy artificially accelerates broad electrification goals or enforces stringent renewable portfolio mandates prior to economic parity.

- Investor Assessment: While the enterprise's systematically balanced generation mix and the presence of established regulatory recovery mechanisms provide a measurable degree of financial buffering, unmitigated physical climate risks remain a recurring and highly unpredictable driver of forward earnings and operating cash flow volatility.

Litigation and Legal Proceedings

- Potential Impact: The enterprise remains subject to ongoing judicial matters, which prominently include complex antitrust litigation specifically related to historical nuclear workforce compensation practices, as well as aggressive environmental citizen suits targeting operational CCR facilities. Adverse judgments in these arenas could ultimately result in the imposition of substantial monetary damages, restrictive injunctive relief, or legally mandated, costly alterations to established daily operating practices.

- Investor Assessment: While standard accounting provisions and balance sheet reserves exist to address probable losses, severely adverse outcomes in these high-profile key cases could precipitate direct financial and operational impacts that significantly exceed current baseline reserve allocations.

urn:contentItem:6JGH-RVD3-RS9K-G472-00000-00Quarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Southern Company reports 226% sequential rise in Quarterly Net Profit Corporate Wire Date: 02 May 2026 04:34 EDT Release Date: April 30, 20262026-05-02T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:48:11ZLexisNexisQuarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Southern Company reports 226% sequential rise in Quarterly Net Profit Corporate Wire Date: 02 May 2026 04:34 EDT Release Date: April 30, 2026

AMERICAN RESULTS

Southern Company (NYSE:SO), announced net profit of $1.4b for the quarter-ended 31 March 2026 [Q1/2026], up 226% from the previous quarter [Q4/2025] and up 1.6% from the year-earlier period [Q1/2025].

Major changes compared with previous corresponding period [Mar 31, 2026 vs Mar 31, 2025]:

Favourable Changes:

- Total revenue was up 8% from $7.8b to $8.4b

Unfavourable Changes:

- EBITDA to Revenues was down from 36.5% to 35.6%

- EBIT to Revenues was down from 19.9% to 18.6%

- Profit before tax to Revenues was down from 19.9% to 18.6%

- Profit after tax to Revenues was down from 17.2% to 16.1%

Note:

- Net profit was steady at $1.4b

- EPS was steady at $1.21.

- Depreciation to Revenues was up from 16.5% to 16.9%

Quarterly Report (Quarter ending 31 Mar 2026)

Quarter-ended31 Mar 202631 Dec 202530 Sep 2025EPS, c121-155Revenue, $ billion8.47.07.8Sequential growth in Revenue %20.3-10.812.2Net Profit, $1.4 billion416 million1.7 billionSequential growth in Net Profit %226-7694

Compared with the Previous Corresponding Period [PCP; Q1/2025], year-over-year [y.o.y.] Revenue was up 8% and Net Profit was up 1.6%.

Quarter-ended31 Mar 202631 Mar 2025EPS, c121121PCP growth in EPS %-17Revenue, $ billion8.47.8PCP growth in Revenue %8.017.0Net Profit, $ billion1.41.3PCP growth in Net Profit %1.618.2

ACTIVITIES:

The Southern Company, a prominent energy holding company listed on the New York Stock Exchange, is a leading provider of electricity and natural gas services in the southeastern United States. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, and distribution of electric power, serving approximately nine million customers through subsidiaries like Alabama Power, Georgia Power, and Mississippi Power. The company also engages in natural gas distribution and owns interests in nuclear and fossil fuel plants, emphasizing reliable and affordable energy solutions. Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy initiatives, particularly solar and wind projects, driven by increasing demand for clean energy and supportive regulatory environments. Additionally, investments in modernizing transmission infrastructure and expanding natural gas operations are fueling growth, positioning the company to capitalize on the transition to a more sustainable energy landscape while maintaining operational efficiency and regulatory compliance.

MARKET POSITION:

The Southern Company (NYSE:SO), the NYSE's 3rd largest Conventional Electricity company by market cap, climbed $8.16 (or 9.2%) in the trailing 12 months to May 01, 2026 to close at $96.71. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which has risen 29.8% over the same period, this is a relative price change of -20.6%. Average daily volume of 5.4 million shares in the trailing year was 1.2 times average daily volume in the previous year.

urn:contentItem:6JGP-35J3-RSH5-M0KF-00000-00Quarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Southern Company reports 20% sequential rise in Quarterly Revenue2026-05-02T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:48:11ZLexisNexisQuarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Southern Company reports 20% sequential rise in Quarterly Revenue

AMERICAN RESULTS

Southern Company (NYSE:SO), announced total revenue of $8.4b for the quarter-ended 31 March 2026 [Q1/2026], up 20% from the previous quarter [Q4/2025] and up 8% from the year-earlier period [Q1/2025].

Major changes compared with previous corresponding period [Mar 31, 2026 vs Mar 31, 2025]:

Favourable Changes:

- Total revenue was up 8% from $7.8b to $8.4b

Unfavourable Changes:

- EBITDA to Revenues was down from 36.5% to 35.6%

- EBIT to Revenues was down from 19.9% to 18.6%

- Profit before tax to Revenues was down from 19.9% to 18.6%

- Profit after tax to Revenues was down from 17.2% to 16.1%

Note:

- Net profit was steady at $1.4b

- EPS was steady at $1.21.

- Depreciation to Revenues was up from 16.5% to 16.9%

Quarterly Report (Quarter ending 31 Mar 2026)

Quarter-ended31 Mar 202631 Dec 202530 Sep 2025EPS, c121-155Revenue, $ billion8.47.07.8Sequential growth in Revenue %20.3-10.812.2Net Profit, $1.4 billion416 million1.7 billionSequential growth in Net Profit %226-7694

Compared with the Previous Corresponding Period [PCP; Q1/2025], year-over-year [y.o.y.] Revenue was up 8% and Net Profit was up 1.6%.

Quarter-ended31 Mar 202631 Mar 2025EPS, c121121PCP growth in EPS %-17Revenue, $ billion8.47.8PCP growth in Revenue %8.017.0Net Profit, $ billion1.41.3PCP growth in Net Profit %1.618.2

ACTIVITIES:

The Southern Company, a prominent energy holding company listed on the New York Stock Exchange, is a leading provider of electricity and natural gas services in the southeastern United States. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, and distribution of electric power, serving approximately nine million customers through subsidiaries like Alabama Power, Georgia Power, and Mississippi Power. The company also engages in natural gas distribution and owns interests in nuclear and fossil fuel plants, emphasizing reliable and affordable energy solutions. Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy initiatives, particularly solar and wind projects, driven by increasing demand for clean energy and supportive regulatory environments. Additionally, investments in modernizing transmission infrastructure and expanding natural gas operations are fueling growth, positioning the company to capitalize on the transition to a more sustainable energy landscape while maintaining operational efficiency and regulatory compliance.

MARKET POSITION:

The Southern Company (NYSE:SO), the NYSE's 3rd largest Conventional Electricity company by market cap, climbed $8.16 (or 9.2%) in the trailing 12 months to May 01, 2026 to close at $96.71. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which has risen 29.8% over the same period, this is a relative price change of -20.6%. Average daily volume of 5.4 million shares in the trailing year was 1.2 times average daily volume in the previous year.
